title: Impossible Foods and Beyond Meat Want to Eliminate Meat in our Future
summary: Impossible Foods founder and CEO Patrick Brown told CNBC that the meat industry is in the midst of a reckoning. “This is why I think people are increasingly aware plant-based products are going to completely replace the animal-based products in the food world within the next 15 years,” he said in CNBC’s “Mad Money” interview. Let the plant based burger wars begin.
description: Impossible Foods founder and CEO Patrick Brown told CNBC that the meat industry is in the midst of a reckoning. As more consumers seek out healthier food options, Brown is convinced that the animal-based market will be obsolete within the next two decades. I’ve met with Ethan Brown (no relation) the CEO of Beyond Meat and his position is about the same. “From a nutritional standpoint our products match the protein quality and content of the animal products that they replace” and “ours is a clear winner from a health and nutrition standpoint,” Impossible Foods CEO Patrick Brown told CNBC. “This is why I think people are increasingly aware plant-based products are going to completely replace the animal-based products in the food world within the next 15 years,” he said in CNBC’s “Mad Money” interview. He added that “That’s our mission. That transformation is inevitable.” Impossible Foods has created yet another partnership with partnership with Starbucks this time to carry the Impossible Breakfast Sandwich at most of their 15,000+ US locations. The Impossible Breakfast Sandwich is a plant-based sausage topped with a cage-free fried egg and aged cheddar cheese on artisanal ciabatta bread. Impossible Foods’ products can also be found in Burger King, Qdoba, White Castle, Red Robin and other restaurants as well as supermarkets including Kroger. But in ads in the Wall St. Journal, New York Times and other papers, Lightlife President Dan Curtin announced a new “Clean Break” campaign to highlight the latest version of their plant-based burger—a vegan patty reformulated to have just eleven ingredients. In an open letter to market leaders Curtin wrote, “Enough with the hyper-processed ingredients, GMOs, unnecessary additives and fillers, and fake blood.” He goes on to write that Lightlife is making a “clean break” from “food tech companies” to use simpler ingredients and methods. Let the plant based burger wars begin.

title: Do Vegan Products Measure Up? (Capturing a Growing Market Event)
summary: Marit Nijman, Business Engagement Scientist, Sensory Science Centre University of Nottingham. - Using sensory techniques to compare plant based and animal based products. The focus of the Sensory Science Centre is on using sensory science and instrumental techniques to understand how we perceive and respond to the sensory characteristics of products.
description: Lifestyle changes, ethical choices and environmental concerns have driven a huge demand for plant and vegan based products in the UK over the last two years. According to Mintel, in January of this year, the UK overtook Germany as the leading country for development of vegan food products. One in six (16%) food products launched in the UK in 2018 had a vegan/no animal ingredients claim, doubling from just 8% in 2015. Overall, one in ten (9%) food products launched in Europe in 2018 had a vegan/no animal ingredients claim, doubling from 5% in 2015. One in three (34%) British meat eaters reduced their meat consumption in the six months to July 2018 following a flexitarian approach, up from 28% who had done so in 2017. To address the challenges associated with developing vegan products, the AFTP a one-day workshop on September 12th 2019 at the University of Nottingham's Sutton Bonington campus and heard from leading experts and meet colleagues from industry. Marit Nijman, Business Engagement Scientist, Sensory Science Centre University of Nottingham. Topic Title: Does your product measure up? - Using sensory techniques to compare plant based and animal based products. Biography: As a business engagement scientist Marit Nijman is responsible for organising and conducting commercial research projects at the Sensory Science Centre at Sutton Bonington Campus. The Sensory Science Centre is based in the Division of Food, Nutrition and Dietetics and has state of the art sensory facilities, designed to ISO Standards (ISO8589:1988). The focus of the Sensory Science Centre is on using sensory science and instrumental techniques to understand how we perceive and respond to the sensory characteristics of products. Marit recently completed her PhD thesis on the role of context when measuring consumers’ emotional responses to sensory properties, which she also conducted at the University of Nottingham. Website:

description: #KFC #KentuckyFriedMiracle #KFCxLightLife KFC's Plant-based Fried Chicken lands in Mississauga for a day! One of the most anticipated fast-foods will be available to the anxiously-awaiting public, but only for one day – and one location. Yes, KFC Canada’s Plant-based Fried Chicken will be making its debut during a limited, one-restaurant, one-day test today, Nov. 27 at the KFC at 6055 Creditview Rd. in Mississauga starting at 10:30 a.m. to 11 p.m., or while supplies last. Developed in response to Canadians’ evolving tastes and preferences for plant-based proteins, KFC’s Plant-Based Fried Chicken is made in Canada by Lightlife. During the limited test release, the Plant-Based Fried Chicken sandwich (available in regular or spicy) will be available for $6.99 or in a meal starting at $8.99, and the Plant-Based Fried Popcorn Chicken bucket is available starting at $3.99. “We believe the Colonel’s famous finger lickin’ good taste should have no boundaries,” said Sam Redman, chief marketing officer, KFC Canada. “So we’ve created several cravable plant-based options for Canadian’s looking for a meat-free meal. One person who had a sneak-peak snack declared it ‘absolutely delicious.’ People are going to be pleasantly surprised by the taste and mouth-feel.” SOURCE: Fast-food giant KFC is the latest company to get on board with plant-based trends in Canada, as they’re introducing vegan fried chicken to one of their Canadian stores! KFC’s Plant-Based Fried Chicken will available for one day only in Mississauga this week, and the customer response will determine whether the Colonel's vegan chicken gets rolled out nationwide in 2020. On Tuesday morning, the chicken chain announced that they’d be introducing a brand-new Plant-Based Fried Chicken in Canada this week. In a statement, the company explained that they wanted to “share the Colonel's legendary finger lickin' good taste with every Canadian,” and have therefore created a plant-based alternative to their classic fried chicken snacks. To create the new vegan treats, KFC Canada partnered up with Lightlife, a leading plant-based protein brand. Together they've produced a meat-free version of the restaurant's traditional Popcorn Chicken and a vegan-version of their Fried Chicken Sandwich. The Plant-Based Fried Chicken Sandwich (available in regular or spicy) will be priced at $6.99 or in a meal starting from $8.99. The Plant-Based Fried Popcorn Chicken Bucket is expected to retail for just $3.99. KFC’s Plant-Based Fried Chicken will make its debut in a limited, one-restaurant, one-day test on Wednesday, November 27 in Mississauga, Ontario. If all goes well, KFC will consider rolling-out the vegan chicken across Canada in 2020. Anybody interested in tasting KFC's new plant-based options are invited to visit KFC at 6055 Creditview Road in Mississauga, Ontario between the hours of 10:30 a.m. to 11p.m., or while supplies last. If you’re not content with trying just one of the new creations, KFC Canada is offering a one-time meal-deal for $10.99, which includes both the plant-based sandwich and the popcorn chicken snack. Earlier this year, KFC U.K. introduced a similar vegan burger in their restaurants, served with egg-free mayonnaise, lettuce, and a vegan chicken fillet coated in KFC’s signature blend of herbs and spices. The “Imposter Burger” sold out in just four days, selling 500 percent more than their average new product. According to KFC Canada's statement on Tuesday, the feedback gathered during the one-day test in Mississauga will determine their plans to roll out the Plant-Based Fried Chicken options nationally in 2020. This follows several other fast-food locations in Canada that have made a move towards more vegetarian-friendly options in 2019. Earlier this year, Tim Hortons launched several plant-based burger and breakfast options, while McDonald's Canada have introduced Beyond Meat Burgers in several Ontario locations. KFC Canada’s Plant-Based Fried Chicken will be available on November 27 from the restaurant located at 6055 Creditview Road in Mississauga, Ontario. title: Veganism and sustainability unstoppable food trends
summary: Alcoholic tea and luxury ready meals are also amongst the growth products on display at the Specialty & Fine Food Fair at London's Olympia. One particular novelty this year is an alcoholic tea, fittingly called 'Noveltea'. While alcoholic tea might be an emerging trend, one trend which continues to be unstoppable is veganism.
description: (3 Sep 2019) LEAD IN Veganism and sustainability continue to be unstoppable trends in the UK food market, from eco friendly CBD drinks through to food wraps made from beeswax. Alcoholic tea and luxury ready meals are also amongst the growth products on display at the Specialty & Fine Food Fair at London's Olympia. STORY-LINE From the finest British cheeses to exotic dishes from South East Asia and beyond - the Specialty & Fine Food Fair at Olympia in Kensington London continues to showcase top quality artisan and luxury brands as it celebrates its 20th anniversary this year. And as always emerging and innovative food and drink concepts are given a major platform too. One particular novelty this year is an alcoholic tea, fittingly called 'Noveltea'. Described as the first brand in the world to cold-brew and infuse tea with a matching spirit it was created by two Newcastle University graduates who raised money through crowdfunding. "It's completely innovative so we essentially introduced a brand new product category entirely to the drinks trade. People have been drinking Irish coffees for years so why not alcoholic tea?", says Katie Gorwits, Sales and Account Manager of Noveltea. She goes on to explain that they have three different alcoholic tea blends inspired by different countries around the world: "Our British based one is Earl Grey Tea with a London Dry Gin, we've got our Moroccan based one that's a Moroccan Green Mint Tea with a Caribbean White Rum and then our Chinese based one is an Oolong Tea with Scotch Whisky." Noveltea originated in 2016 and is already being stocked in major stores such as Fenwick, Harrods and John Lewis in the UK as well as being exported to China and Germany. While alcoholic tea might be an emerging trend, one trend which continues to be unstoppable is veganism. After five years running an award-winning London restaurant, The Brook now creates high end plant based foods and delivers them to people's doors to enjoy at home. Founder Thea Brook says veganism is growing at such a fast pace it now has many sub sections within it - from high end to junk food: "I think that veganism's getting big enough now that you have a luxury segment within it, or maybe not even quite yet but it's definitely going that way. You've got sections to the vegan food market that are more about raw foods and health foods and then there's a lot of vegan junk food that's coming onto the market which people love." Another trend here is to have restaurant quality, high end food delivered to your door which you can even pass off as a delicious meal you cooked yourself. Not long ago frozen meals at a fine food fair would be unheard of - but this year 'Cook's high end ready meals sit centre stage of the exhibition hall. Alex Dimmock, Concessions Manager at Cook says: "Customers want quite high end, premium, high quality products that they can present at home as if they've done it themselves. So with Cook in particular we can make the food that you can produce on your kitchen table at home, in your own pots and it looks like it's been home made by yourself. And you still know you're going to get that high quality product." Another trend which is also growing is CBD and hemp based food and drink products. CBD (Cannabidiol) is an active ingredient in cannabis derived from the hemp plant. It's legal in the UK, provided it's extracted from industrial hemp which is EU approved. Cannasa claims to be the newest CBD drink to hit the UK drinks market. Julie Rickaby is Director of one such product called 'Bumble Wrap': Find out more about AP Archive: Twitter: Facebook: ​​ Instagram: You can license this story through AP Archive:

title: Vegan Food: Would You Try a 'Steak' Made of Plants?
summary: The plant-based food industry is booming. Yeung says millennials are driving the plant-based movement. Everyone, every day is an opportunity to make a difference.” Subscribe to our YouTube channel: QUICKTAKE ON SOCIAL: Follow QuickTake on Twitter: twitter.com/quicktake Like QuickTake on Facebook: facebook.com/quicktake Follow QuickTake on Instagram: instagram.com/quicktake Subscribe to our newsletter: Email us at quicktakenews@gmail.com QuickTake by Bloomberg is a global news network delivering up-to-the-minute analysis on the biggest news, trends and ideas for a new generation of leaders.
description: The plant-based food industry is booming. The U.S retail market for plant-based foods is worth almost $4.5 billion, according to the Good Food Institute. Bloomberg QuickTake's Rosalie e’Silva speaks with David Yeung, the Hong Kong-based entrepreneur who started a global movement to cut meat consumption, and tests out some vegan products. Yeung says millennials are driving the plant-based movement. “A huge part of this change in fact is coming from millennials. They are much more informed about not just food, but everything.” And with that growth comes new and innovative products. Yeung says, “The industry has really changed a lot, even as recent as the last 3 years. We’re seeing so many new products.” According to Yeung, plant-based meat, cheese, yogurt and snacks are some of the best-sellers. “Shifting from meats to plant-based, you are indeed making a big impact on the planet. Our goal is to that anyone who comes in can find great food, delicious and healthy food and it's for everyone.” He adds, ” Ultimately we want to broaden the base of people consuming plant-based foods. title: Veganism and sustainability unstoppable food trends
summary: Alcoholic tea and luxury ready meals are also amongst the growth products on display at the Specialty & Fine Food Fair at London's Olympia. One particular novelty this year is an alcoholic tea, fittingly called 'Noveltea'. While alcoholic tea might be an emerging trend, one trend which continues to be unstoppable is veganism.
description: (3 Sep 2019) LEAD IN Veganism and sustainability continue to be unstoppable trends in the UK food market, from eco friendly CBD drinks through to food wraps made from beeswax. Alcoholic tea and luxury ready meals are also amongst the growth products on display at the Specialty & Fine Food Fair at London's Olympia. STORY-LINE From the finest British cheeses to exotic dishes from South East Asia and beyond - the Specialty & Fine Food Fair at Olympia in Kensington London continues to showcase top quality artisan and luxury brands as it celebrates its 20th anniversary this year. And as always emerging and innovative food and drink concepts are given a major platform too. One particular novelty this year is an alcoholic tea, fittingly called 'Noveltea'. Described as the first brand in the world to cold-brew and infuse tea with a matching spirit it was created by two Newcastle University graduates who raised money through crowdfunding. "It's completely innovative so we essentially introduced a brand new product category entirely to the drinks trade. People have been drinking Irish coffees for years so why not alcoholic tea?", says Katie Gorwits, Sales and Account Manager of Noveltea. She goes on to explain that they have three different alcoholic tea blends inspired by different countries around the world: "Our British based one is Earl Grey Tea with a London Dry Gin, we've got our Moroccan based one that's a Moroccan Green Mint Tea with a Caribbean White Rum and then our Chinese based one is an Oolong Tea with Scotch Whisky." Noveltea originated in 2016 and is already being stocked in major stores such as Fenwick, Harrods and John Lewis in the UK as well as being exported to China and Germany. While alcoholic tea might be an emerging trend, one trend which continues to be unstoppable is veganism. After five years running an award-winning London restaurant, The Brook now creates high end plant based foods and delivers them to people's doors to enjoy at home. Founder Thea Brook says veganism is growing at such a fast pace it now has many sub sections within it - from high end to junk food: "I think that veganism's getting big enough now that you have a luxury segment within it, or maybe not even quite yet but it's definitely going that way. You've got sections to the vegan food market that are more about raw foods and health foods and then there's a lot of vegan junk food that's coming onto the market which people love." Another trend here is to have restaurant quality, high end food delivered to your door which you can even pass off as a delicious meal you cooked yourself. Not long ago frozen meals at a fine food fair would be unheard of - but this year 'Cook's high end ready meals sit centre stage of the exhibition hall. Alex Dimmock, Concessions Manager at Cook says: "Customers want quite high end, premium, high quality products that they can present at home as if they've done it themselves. So with Cook in particular we can make the food that you can produce on your kitchen table at home, in your own pots and it looks like it's been home made by yourself. And you still know you're going to get that high quality product." Another trend which is also growing is CBD and hemp based food and drink products. CBD (Cannabidiol) is an active ingredient in cannabis derived from the hemp plant. It's legal in the UK, provided it's extracted from industrial hemp which is EU approved. Cannasa claims to be the newest CBD drink to hit the UK drinks market. Julie Rickaby is Director of one such product called 'Bumble Wrap': Find out more about AP Archive: Twitter: Facebook: ​​ Instagram: You can license this story through AP Archive:

title: Should you go vegan? | Bork
summary: Reducing meat intake is actually the largest single way a person can reduce their environmental impact, not just in terms of greenhouse gases but also to reduce deforestation, land use and water consumption. A journal published in Environmental Research Letters found that eating a plant-based diet has three times more impact than washing your clothes in cold water, four times more than hang-drying clothes and eight times more than upgrading light bulbs. Whatever the numbers, interest is at an all-time high – Just Eat notes it observed demand for meat-free food increasing by a staggering 987% in 2017, with market researcher Mintel showing the UK market for meat-free foods was worth £572 million last year, up from £539 million only two years before.
description: Reducing meat intake is actually the largest single way a person can reduce their environmental impact, not just in terms of greenhouse gases but also to reduce deforestation, land use and water consumption. A journal published in Environmental Research Letters found that eating a plant-based diet has three times more impact than washing your clothes in cold water, four times more than hang-drying clothes and eight times more than upgrading light bulbs. What was once a fringe movement on the edge of society is undoubtedly going mainstream, although the exact figures vary depending on who you ask – The Vegan Society found there are 600,000 British vegans in 2018, four times more than there were in 2014 and now making up 1.16% of the entire population. Meanwhile, research from CompareTheMarket claims there are as many as 3.5 million people enjoying animal product-free meals across the country. Whatever the numbers, interest is at an all-time high – Just Eat notes it observed demand for meat-free food increasing by a staggering 987% in 2017, with market researcher Mintel showing the UK market for meat-free foods was worth £572 million last year, up from £539 million only two years before. Google Trends shows a continuous surge in interest in vegan foods over the last decade, as evidenced in the graph below, with the number of searches quadrupling in the five years between 2012 and 2017. But why does this matter? What does your choice of dinner have to do with energy and emissions? title: Ethics of Plant Based Foods (Capturing a Growing Market)
summary: One in six (16%) food products launched in the UK in 2018 had a vegan/no animal ingredients claim, doubling from just 8% in 2015. Overall, one in ten (9%) food products launched in Europe in 2018 had a vegan/no animal ingredients claim, doubling from 5% in 2015. Biography: Mark Driscoll is Founder and Director of Tasting the Future, a not for profit sustainable food systems consultancy.
description: Lifestyle changes, ethical choices and environmental concerns have driven a huge demand for plant and vegan based products in the UK over the last two years. According to Mintel, in January of this year, the UK overtook Germany as the leading country for development of vegan food products. One in six (16%) food products launched in the UK in 2018 had a vegan/no animal ingredients claim, doubling from just 8% in 2015. Overall, one in ten (9%) food products launched in Europe in 2018 had a vegan/no animal ingredients claim, doubling from 5% in 2015. One in three (34%) British meat eaters reduced their meat consumption in the six months to July 2018 following a flexitarian approach, up from 28% who had done so in 2017. To address the challenges associated with developing vegan products, the AFTP a one-day workshop on September 12th 2019 at the University of Nottingham's Sutton Bonington campus and heard from leading experts and meet colleagues from industry. Mark Driscoll, Founder and Director, Tasting the Future Topic Title: Raw Materials – Ethical and organic sourcing of ingredients e.g. palm oil, cocoa, walking the talk - does it matter? Biography: Mark Driscoll is Founder and Director of Tasting the Future, a not for profit sustainable food systems consultancy. He is a passion advocate for the need for food systems change. He has 25 years experience of working with businesses, governments and civil society organisations on solutions that address some of the key social and environmental challenges confronting our global food system. He has developed and led large sustainable food programmes with organisations including WWF and Forum for the Future, focussing on policy and practice at the intersection of health, nutrition and sustainability. Mark graduated from Wye College (London University) with a degree in agriculture and environmental science. He sits on a number of advisory boards for business, government and non-profit organisations. He also writes and blogs extensively on issues and trends impacting on the food system. When not working, Mark likes to reconnect is head, hands and heart with the soil, through his passion for growing his own vegetables! Website:

summary: There are many other products that have been designed to target the elderly,... This is called self-focused consumption." In fact, because of the growing elderly population, the consumer market for senior citizens, excluding financial products,... #elderly #market #industry Arirang News Facebook:
description: 식품부터 가전제품까지...커지는 실버산업 South Korea's elderly are more willing to their invest their precious time and money on enjoying themselves compared to the past. With the shift in consumption trends, instead of being largely overlooked, the market for products aimed at the elderly is rapidly expanding. Hong Yoo reports. In the past, senior citizens had a hard time keeping up with the latest consumption trends,... especially in electronic devices such as smartphones. But now, around 80 percent of people in their 60s carry smartphones,... especially ones that have been customized to be easier for older people to use. There are many other products that have been designed to target the elderly,... who have been called the "new silver generation" in Korea. One of these is a smartphone which has content tailored to the older generation,... such as teuroteu music and word quiz games. Also, it comes with a pre-installed dementia prevention app... that helps users check their mental health once in a while. It has a wide 6-inch screen and is very user friendly. "I use smartphones to watch videos on YouTube. I watch the news with my phone and I also use Kakaotalk to chat with people." There are also AI speakers made for elderly citizens living alone... which they can talk to and feel less lonely. The device can detect a user's emotions when they're having a conversation. Even though it's not a human companion, the elderly say it helps them feel less lonely. Even more, they come equipped with games designed to prevent dementia. Users can ask the speaker to start the quiz,... where they will have to answer 4 questions based on memorization. "Aging well" is a term that could sum up the latest consumption trends among the elderly. They are willing to spend more on healthy foods,... and other premium goods that are organic and eco-friendly. "The new silver generation, people who have the money and time to invest in themselves, consume in order to reward themselves and be happy. This is called self-focused consumption." In fact, because of the growing elderly population, the consumer market for senior citizens, excluding financial products,... is expected to reach 60-point-8 billion U.S. dollars in 2020, according to the Korea Health Industry Development Institute. Hong Yoo, Arirang News. #elderly #market #industry Arirang News Facebook:

title: Veganism trend impacts global trade
summary: In the UK, it is estimated that one in eight people has ditched meat from their diet, with many more choosing only to eat meat occasionally. As a result, meat substitute products are growing in popularity and new favourites are emerging. In some cases, this may mean looking further afield for crops and meat substitutes which can fulfil the growing demand.
description: Reported today on City AM For the full article visit: As more and more people go vegan for the environment, trade routes for food and drink are expandingStatistics show that the number of vegans and vegetarians is steadily growing. In the UK, it is estimated that one in eight people has ditched meat from their diet, with many more choosing only to eat meat occasionally. The trend coincides with an increase in awareness of the health benefits of cutting out animal products and ties in also with the growing awareness of the environmental impact of consuming meat. As a result, meat substitute products are growing in popularity and new favourites are emerging. For example, jackfruit, a fleshy fruit grown in south-east Asia, Brazil and Africa, is becoming a popular ingredient in vegan meals and can be cooked as a great alternative to pulled pork.What does the rise in veganism mean for imports and exports?Producers are adapting to changing trends in consumer tastes. In some cases, this may mean looking further afield for crops and meat substitutes which can fulfil the growing demand. As a result, many in the industry are starting to import from across the world and are working with new partners in different countries. Together with the impact of Brexit, which is seeing many businesses look further afield to address the uncertainty of future trade agreements with the EU, many organisations are now working with suppliers and partners in previously unfamiliar countries and with new currencies.Managing the global supply chainBuilding relationships across the globe to address the growing trend of veganism makes a lot of sense for businesses. title: The Rise of Free-From Packaged Foods in Western Europe
summary: In Western Europe, although we see a general decline in certain mature categories, such as baked goods, meat and dairy, there is rapid growth in the market for free-from gluten, meat, and dairy products. The rise of free-from food products has been a key trend throughout 2017. Once considered niche categories, the free-from categories are now going mainstream and have more shelf-space.
description: In Western Europe, although we see a general decline in certain mature categories, such as baked goods, meat and dairy, there is rapid growth in the market for free-from gluten, meat, and dairy products. The rise of free-from food products has been a key trend throughout 2017. Once considered niche categories, the free-from categories are now going mainstream and have more shelf-space. The main drivers responsible for this growth are the rise of the health conscious consumer, growing manufacturer activity and consumer awareness. The biggest three markets for free-from in Western Europe in value terms are Italy, the United Kingdom and Germany.

title: Food and Foodservice Trends from the National Restaurant Association Show 2019
summary: In packaged food, protein in traditionally low-protein categories was a clear trend, with companies like Trident Seafoods using fish in pasta to up the protein content. Meat substitutes also continues to be a trend, with well known companies like Impossible featured prominently at the show. However, meat substitute companies are expanding into other ingredients such as jackfruit.
description: Euromonitor International's research team was at the 2019 National Restaurant Association show to observe trends in the food and foodservice industries. In packaged food, protein in traditionally low-protein categories was a clear trend, with companies like Trident Seafoods using fish in pasta to up the protein content. Meat substitutes also continues to be a trend, with well known companies like Impossible featured prominently at the show. However, meat substitute companies are expanding into other ingredients such as jackfruit. In consumer foodservice, personalisation continues to be a trend, evident in self-pour bars. In delivery, companies are focused on sustainable, durable containers.

title: NEW VEGAN PRODUCTS COMING IN 2021
summary: There are tons of new plant-based products coming in 2021. We will likely see these vegan donuts in early 2021. Bonus: Pizza Hut The Pizza Hut locations in Australia and the UK pizza have expanded to include a full vegan menu with Violife vegan cheese, nuggs, VEaGN STUFFED CRUST, cheesecake, and more.
description: There are tons of new plant-based products coming in 2021. As vegan diets continue to grow in popularity, we are seeing many companies jumping on the plant-based bandwagon. From fast-food chains adding to their menu to new versions of our favorite childhood snacks, there's a lot to look forward to next year. COME SAY HI! Instagram: Tik Tok: GEAR I USE IN MY VEGAN KITCHEN: Vitamix Blender - Large wood cutting board - Recommended Side Hustles: ► Ibotta $20 sign-up bonus - ► Fetch Rewards get $2 when you snap your first receipt - Here are some of the new vegan products coming out in 2021 that I am most excited for! Jack In The Box is testing their first sandwich with plant-based chicken! It's called the 'Unchicken sandwich.' Vegan Chocolate-dipped banana ice cream bars are launching at Costco and are only 120 calories per bar!! They are made with bananas, dates, coconut milk, and organic chocolate and are available in 10-bar packages for $9.99 Dunkin' Donuts Confirms they are working on a vegan donut - the CEO, David Hoffmann, made the announcement at a recent company shareholder’s meeting. Dunkin has already launched oat milk nationwide and beyond sausage, so this news was highly anticipated. We will likely see these vegan donuts in early 2021. Vegan Babybel cheese! Bel group is developing dairy-free options for each of its core brands, which include Babybel, The Laughing Cow, and more. The company aims to launch plant-based mini Babybel cheeses in the United States next year Field Roast launches 5 new vegan cheese varieties and will be available to retailers nationwide very soon! Including 1,000 Walmart locations for a suggested retail price of $5.99 Daiya is offering bulk packs of vegan cheese at Costco! Daiya says that they are using a new recipe that's made with chickpea protein and have a better flavor, melt, and stretch than you’d expect from any dairy-free cheese. Impossible Milk! Impossible milk is still in development and will be nothing like other plant-based milks. The CEO of Impossible Foods, Pat Brown, says that the company wants the beverage to taste like the real thing. They note that it will likely be made from soy, as soy has a strong nutrient profile. The company is doubling their research and development over the next 12 months for the creation of new plant-based products, like the Impossible Milk. They have raised $700 million this year in funding. Bonus: Just folded Egg! The company 'Just' is launching their 'Folded Egg' at more than 17,000 retail distribution sites in the United States by the end of the year. Expanding to Kroger, Walmart, Albertsons, Safeway and other stores nationwide. The reviews of this product say that it is extraordinary and convenient! Bonus: Pizza Hut The Pizza Hut locations in Australia and the UK pizza have expanded to include a full vegan menu with Violife vegan cheese, nuggs, VEaGN STUFFED CRUST, cheesecake, and more. Currently, the vegan menu is only available in Australia and the UK, but with growing U.S. demand, we may also see the menu expanded nationally in 2021. There has been no official statement regarding this, but rumors and wishful thinking! That's all of my favorite new vegan products coming in 2021 that you might not have known about! title: Korean shoppers worry about surging food prices amid drought and AI outbreak
summary: 6월 소비자물가 APK Consumer prices continue to rise in June. rose zero-point-nine percent and one-point-nine percent respectively. Energy costs fell one-point-six percent,...
description: 6월 소비자물가 APK Consumer prices continue to rise in June. The trend is largely driven by surging food prices on the back of a prolonged drought and an avian influenza outbreak. Oh Soo-young looks beyond the digits. Korea's consumer prices in June rose one-point-nine percent from the year before, according to data from Statistics Korea. Much of the inflation was attributed to spiking fresh food prices in the wake of a recent drought and an Avian Influenza outbreak. The price of eggs alone have jumped almost 70 percent on-year. "Take this box of eggs for example. It costs 7 U.S. dollars. This time last year,... you could have bought almost twice as many eggs for the same price." Prices for agricultural, livestock and seafood increased seven-point-six percent,... while industrial products and services, which include rental and insurance fees,... rose zero-point-nine percent and one-point-nine percent respectively. Energy costs fell one-point-six percent,... due to a sharp drop in electricity prices. Core inflation, which excludes volatile food and energy costs,.. rose by a more modest one-point-four percent... compared to the overall growth in CPI. But with sky-high grocery prices,.. the average consumer is feeling a pinch in their wallets. "Eggs, tofu, vegetables are all staple foods for the average citizen and it's very worrying that the prices keep going up." "I've noticed the prices of food and amenities keep getting higher, so I'm being forced to cut down on other expenditures." Experts worry that consumers may react by tightening their purse strings,... further worsening the country's low consumption rate. Recent data shows consumer sentiment in June-- or confidence in the Korean economy-- surged to a six-year high. However,... experts say this is largely due to a base effect and expectations of new policies from the Moon Jae-in administration. "Lingering uncertainties such as the impact of U.S. interest rate hikes, the high ratio of household debt and the employment situation in the country is likely to continue to put a damper on spending." The expert said we need to wait and see whether there'll be a significant recovery in consumption,... but if the government's supplementary budget proposal to create 110-thousand jobs is passed, bolstering income and employment stability,... this could provide a much needed boost to the domestic economy. title: Halal foods: From burgers to Burmese noodles
summary: The Halal Gems Street Eats festival reflects both the food diversity and the trends popular here in East London's fashionable Shoreditch area. "Muslims are hugely interested in foodie culture. You're eating," says the festival's founder Zohra Khaku.
description: (31 Jul 2017) LEADIN: From fried cheese burgers to Burmese soup noodles, a festival in London is celebrating the diversity of the UK capital's halal food. The Street Eats festival is putting the spotlight on the UK's growing appetite for halal, which is seeing something of a foodie revolution. STORYLINE: Beef burgers with fried cheese and chips, Middle Eastern olive oil and spices for dipping bread, tacos with different toppings. You can travel the world at this London food festival - and every last bite is halal. The Halal Gems Street Eats festival reflects both the food diversity and the trends popular here in East London's fashionable Shoreditch area. It's a part of the capital where lots of Muslims live and it is also has a thriving and ever-changing food culture, with street food and pop up restaurants attracting plenty of young Londoners. "Muslims are hugely interested in foodie culture. So if you imagine, in the halal conscious lifestyle, if you're not drinking, what are you doing? You're eating," says the festival's founder Zohra Khaku. "So really, Muslims eat out a lot and statistically we spend a lot more money on eating out than non-Muslims do. So the idea really is to make sure that halal-conscious diners a have place to go to, and here we are." The word 'halal' is most commonly used in reference to meat, but it actually refers to any object or action which is permissible for Muslims, as set out in the Quran. In the case of meat, Islamic law requires that animals are alive and healthy at the time of slaughter, are killed by hand, have prayers of thanks recited over them, and have all blood drained from the carcass. As well as using Halal produce, the festival is showcasing fusion foods that combine Middle Eastern influences with the latest London foodie fads. One stand attracting those with a sweet tooth is Darlish Creamery. From sweet cream and sour cherry to chocolate and halwa, this mobile ice cream parlour draws heavily on Persian Muslim culture. Laura Dawson, the founder of Darlish Persian Creamery, says she blends chocolate with traditional Iranian halwa which is made from sesame seeds. "Chocolate and halwa is going on, and this one is just inspired by the sesame candy that we'd normally have with coffee or as a sweet treat. Everyone will say, 'oh I remember halwa when I was a child', so we made a chocolate with some chunks of halwa in there," she says. Muslims make up 4.8 percent of the UK population, according to the 2011 census by the Office of National Statistics. The Muslim Council of Britain says that the UK halal food industry is worth over three billion UK pounds (approx. 3.9 billion US dollars). Asna Qamar, who's originally from Pakistan, is offering a halal take on the popular Burmese noodle dish Khao suey. "It's essentially a bowl of noodles with a coconut soup, slow cooked chicken or pulled beef and lots of crunchy things that go on top, from a homemade chilli sauce, a prawn crumble, we have spring onions and lots of lime and lemon," she says. Zohra Khaku says there is much more to Muslim food than tradition. "Muslims love eating different types of cuisine, and it's actually quite novel, the different types of things you can find in London, so here at Street Eats for example we've got things from curry to halloumi fries and burgers and all sorts of things. It's really very exciting." Halloumi is a firm, chewy white cheese that hails from Cyprus originally and is made from both cows' and sheep's milk. It lends itself particularly well to grilling and frying. And it's not just Muslims who are enjoying all the Halal delights on offer. title: Penthouses and hotel suites get vegan makeover
summary: (22 Apr 2019) LEADIN Meghan Markle will reportedly paint her baby's nursery with vegan paint - and now hotels and luxury apartments are also opting for animal friendly products. With the trend for vegan food on the increase attention is now turning to vegan properties. Even Meghan Markle - a famed animal lover - is reported to be painting her baby's nursery at Frogmore Cottage, Windsor in vegan paint, which doesn't contains animal by-products.
description: (22 Apr 2019) LEADIN Meghan Markle will reportedly paint her baby's nursery with vegan paint - and now hotels and luxury apartments are also opting for animal friendly products. With the trend for vegan food on the increase attention is now turning to vegan properties. STORYLINE Sit down at most dinner parties these days and the chances are at least one person will be vegan - or at least considering a more vegan lifestyle. But in this luxury penthouse in Hammersmith, London, not only is everything on the dining table vegan, the entire interior design - from the furnishings to the wallpaper - is vegan too. The Marquis House penthouse, part of a new Berkeley St George development at Sovereign Court, went on the market at almost 5 million British pounds and has just sold to a lucky new owner. Its interior, part of a wider movement towards more animal friendly foods and products, was created by the CEO and Founder of MM Furnishings, Itala Santos. Her family run business specialises in luxury vegan furnishings (most of her family members are vegan) and she says she'd like to think it was this cruelty free approach which helped win her the contract: "You're providing something that aesthetically looks very, very opulent and glamorous, which it does, and is suiting to a penthouse finish but yet it has a wonderful ethical belief and understanding behind it," she explains. As well as using paint which isn't tested on animals Santos also avoided duck down pillows and duvets, cushions with feather fillings and throws made from sheep wool or mohair. She also looked for alternatives to materials such as leather and suede for features such as the large bedroom headboards: "Instead of going for obviously non ethical related products we chose an absolutely gorgeous burnt orange faux suede headboard, we also had faux suede in the master bedroom in a lovely kind of sky blue." And even the artworks at Marquis House are vegan: "It was all about finding suppliers that would have artists to replicate designs that we had chosen and those artists would then utilise specifically vegan paint and brushes as well to be able to create these lovely pieces that we've placed in the property," says Santos. She says one item which has long been associated with luxury is fur, but that faux alternatives can still look and feel just as opulent: "I've always believed you do not have to jeopardise in quality and in luxury by choosing an ethical approach of materials." There are an estimated 540,000 vegans in the UK according to The Vegan Society, so it's no surprise vegan interiors are now more in demand. Even Meghan Markle - a famed animal lover - is reported to be painting her baby's nursery at Frogmore Cottage, Windsor in vegan paint, which doesn't contains animal by-products. When asked how she'd respond if Meghan hired her to make her home 100 per cent vegan Santos says: "I think I would fall off my chair...I think any interior designer would jump at the chance." But it's not just private properties which are getting vegan makeovers. In line with an increased demand for vegan food options in hotels - one hotel has gone a step further. Claiming to be a world first, the Hilton Bankside Hotel in London has created a 'Vegan Suite'. And the experience is animal friendly from the moment you check in. The key holder you receive for the Vegan Suite, and even the chairs you sit on at Guest Relations, may look like leather - but they're actually made from a material called Pinatex, which comes from pineapples. And once in the suite itself everything is free from animal cruelty or products. Find out more about AP Archive: Twitter: Facebook: ​​ Instagram: You can license this story through AP Archive:

description: Tesagan Gin Ge festival Difference between Je, Vegan and Vegetarian : Here is a quick definition and comparisons between Je, Vegetarian and Vegan. JE: actually comes from “Jain”. They don’t consume meat, poultry, seafood, or any animal products (eggs, milk…). Furthermore, jay food excludes 4 kinds of pungent vegetables; including garlic, onion, chinese single-bulbed garlic and chinese chive. VEGETARIAN : Refuse to eat meat but often may consume other animal products such as milk, eggs and cheese. In Thai people often call this “Mung sa-wi-rat” VEGAN : impose stricter rules onto themselves and refrain from eating animal produces like milk, butter and eggs and they will avoid any other use of animal products.

title: Are you ready to eat insects? Save the planet, eat bugs | UNTV
summary: One such strategy that's growing more popular, yet is still unthinkable for many is the consumption of insects. Gourmands concur and say the insects are a treat. Indeed, the market for edible insects is growing in North America, even as it's long established in many cultures throughout the world.
description: WATCH | In an era in which concern over the world's climate only continues to grow deeper, experts are regularly encouraging extreme and perhaps even creative action. One such strategy that's growing more popular, yet is still unthinkable for many is the consumption of insects. This year's Earth Day is being marked Monday (April 22), and advocates of edible insects are singing the environmental benefits of sprinkling your dishes with crickets, grasshoppers and ants. Gourmands concur and say the insects are a treat. Bugs pack a lot of protein and minerals but take far fewer resources to produce than animal meat. Indeed, the market for edible insects is growing in North America, even as it's long established in many cultures throughout the world. title: ASDA VEGAN GROCERY HAUL - WHAT I EAT IN A DAY PLANT BASED. Lara Joanna Jarvis
summary: We're real meat eaters, but we're trying to go vegan - plant based - here's what I eat in a day and new vegan food at Asda in this grocery haul. We're trying the vegan diet as a lifestyle change, not as a diet, so it might be sometimes we do it as flexitarians, sometimes vegetarian, pescatarian etc - but probably not fully vegan as we still wear leather shoes. I've loved eating vegan food, but I was not expecting the symptoms of such bloating and painful tummy!!
description: ARE YOU DOING VEGANUARY?? We're real meat eaters, but we're trying to go vegan - plant based - here's what I eat in a day and new vegan food at Asda in this grocery haul. We're trying the vegan diet as a lifestyle change, not as a diet, so it might be sometimes we do it as flexitarians, sometimes vegetarian, pescatarian etc - but probably not fully vegan as we still wear leather shoes. Whatever you choose to eat is your choice and no one should ever judge you on that - we've been big meat eaters but we really wanted to go plant based as a lifestyle rather than a challenge. Have you been doing #veganuary - let me know what your fave recipes are!! I've loved eating vegan food, but I was not expecting the symptoms of such bloating and painful tummy!! I think I might have an allergy to soya and nightshade vegetables - nightmare!! Thanks so much for watching, come and find me on Instagram if you want to see my outfits of the day and more of what I get up to. www.instagram.com/larajoannajarvis Thanks for supporting me and being a part of this amazing community, it would mean the world to me if you would like to subscribe to see more from me, I appreciate every single one of you! description: How good is popular UK supermarket Sainsbury's if you happen to be one of those awesome vegan people I keep hearing about..? QUICK DISCLAIMER: The Mac And Cheese w/Jackfruit is NOT vegan SOZ! Vivo Life protein: Code: HENCH10 for 10% off! If you enjoyed this video you will LOVE these: Thanks for watching. Don't forget to SUBSCRIBE and HIT THE BELL so you are notified of new uploads! *** Help Support My Activism *** 1-to-1 personal training sessions and online coaching contact me at Currently offering bespoke nutrition/workout plans at £30 each or both for £50! Skype/telephone consultations £50/hr (or part thereof) Hench Herbivore clothing & merchandise: Donate via Patreon: *** About Me *** I'm Paul Kerton. Personal Trainer, Vegan Nutritionist and Public Speaker. Not so long ago, in a misguided attempt to try and grow muscle as quickly as possible, I ended up eating more animal products than probably anyone you've ever met. Through research I came to learn the deleterious effects that eating even quite trivial amounts of animal products has on one's health and through personal experience I found that far from being a hindrance to sports performance, a well planned vegan diet is actually optimal! Then with an open heart I looked at how we treat animals in order for them to end up on our plates and it shook me to the core. I could not believe that for so long I fell for the lies that others tell us, that we tell ourselves, in order that we perpetuate this destructive behaviour. Destructive to ourselves, the beautiful, intelligent sentient beings that are here with us and indeed our planet. To try and put right my part in all of this I was compelled to overcome my fear of being filmed for YouTube and television, appearing on the radio and more recently public speaking. On a spiritual level I've come to realise that my fellow man too is suffering. If I won't see harm come to a chicken I've never met then why would I be unkind to my brothers? If someone is being aggressive or unkind toward me, surely the suffering inflicted on them in the past is simply spilling over? title: The perfect Veganuary plant-based Ploughmans sandwich by Exceedingly Vegan
summary: Vegan Ploughman's Sandwich, recipe and video by Philipp Ertl of Exceedingly Vegan. Then add slices of the vegan cheese you made. Top with vegan mayo, tahini or cashew creme (recipe here).
description: Vegan Ploughman's Sandwich, recipe and video by Philipp Ertl of Exceedingly Vegan. If you’re looking for work lunch ideas then this recipe is for you! This vegan Ploughman’s sandwich recipe is easy to make and is perfect to take to work. You only need a few ingredients, a blender, and stove and you will be ready to make your own plant-based version of the traditional Ploughman’s sandwich in no time. The vegan cheese is made mainly from cashews and you would be surprised how much it tastes like cheese! If you want you can also add some vegan mayo or cashew sour cream for added flavour. Bringing lunch to work not only saves money but this way you have more control over your diet. This vegan Ploughman’s sandwich can be stored easily using Addis Clip & Go Roll & Wrap containers and is great for storing your lunches or bringing food with you on the go! INGREDIENTS (4 sandwiches): Step 1: vegan cashew cheese recipe: 75g unsalted cashew nuts 150ml water 4 tbsp corn starch 3 1/2 tbsp nutritional yeast 2 tbsp lemon juice 1 tbsp olive oil 1 tsp salt Step 2: other Ploughman's ingredients: Small baguette or bread Pickle Tomatoes Lettuce Vegan mayo, tahini or cashew creme METHOD: Step 1: Start with making your own cashew cheese by adding all ingredients into a blender cup and then blend for at least 1 minute until you get a very smooth cream. There is no need to soak up the cashew nuts, most smoothie blenders will do the job perfectly. Then pour the cheese cream into a sauce pan and heat up to to medium to high heat. Make sure you stir constantly in order not to burn the cream. After a while the starch reacts with the heat and quickly thickens up and the cream will turn into a thick paste. When that happens take it off the heat and transfer it into a kitchen container (like the one from Addis I am using in my video). Push the paste into the container with a spoon so that you get an even surface. Then move the container to the fridge and let set for about 4 hours for it to turn from a thick paste into vegan cheese. The longer the better to be honest and I find that the constistency is even better after at least a day. This vegan cashew cheese should last for about 1 week, though mine never lasted that long hehe. Step 2: Cut a baguette in half (or you can also use normal bread) and spread pickle on top (you can find that in any UK supermarket). Then add slices of the vegan cheese you made. Top with sliced tomatoes and shredded iceberg lettuce. Top with vegan mayo, tahini or cashew creme (recipe here). Either enjoy instantly or take to work with you for a delicious vegan lunch everyone will envy you for. Enjoy! Philipp Ertl is a recipe developer and founder of Exceedingly Vegan. His goal is to bring everyone back to the table with his delicious creations, vegans, flexitarians and omnivores. You can follow him on Instagram and Facebook.

title: How Tea Time Came to England
summary: There’s nothing quite as quintessentially British as a cuppa tea. But how did the Chinese tradition make its way into the British consciousness? Soon, everyone wanted to be just like the Queen and sales of tea began to skyrocket.
description: There’s nothing quite as quintessentially British as a cuppa tea. But how did the Chinese tradition make its way into the British consciousness? It’s all thanks to Catherine of Braganza, a Portuguese royal who married England’s King Charles II in 1662. When she arrived in England, she brought with her loose leaves and spices in a set of crates labeled “Transporte de Ervas Aromatics,” or T.E.A. Soon, everyone wanted to be just like the Queen and sales of tea began to skyrocket. The rest, as they say, is history. summary: Pat McAuley is a Boston-based serial entrepreneur, author, speaker, and startup consultant for ethical/plant-based companies. He is now an outspoken advocate of the health and environmental benefits of plant-based living.
description: NOTE FROM TED: While some viewers may find the nutrition advice provided in this talk to be helpful, please do not look to this talk for medical advice. We've flagged this talk, which was filmed at a TEDx event, because it appears to fall outside TEDx's content guidelines. This talk contains strong assertions about a specific diet that lack sufficient scientific evidence for general prescription. TEDx events are independently organized by volunteers. The guidelines we give TEDx organizers are described in more detail here: Pat McAuley describes the impact that food has on his life and why he is an advocate of the health and environmental benefits of plant-based living. Pat McAuley is a Boston-based serial entrepreneur, author, speaker, and startup consultant for ethical/plant-based companies. He has started and successfully run companies in the tech space and food and beverage industries and has extensive experience building brands from scratch. After a plant-based diet changed his life, Pat shifted his focus to building and supporting businesses in the plant-based space. He is now an outspoken advocate of the health and environmental benefits of plant-based living. Pat is the host of the popular Eat Green Make Green Podcast and is currently working on opening one of the nation’s first fully plant-based beer halls, Rewild. This talk was given at a TEDx event using the TED conference format but independently organized by a local community. Learn more at

description: The number of vegans is on the rise across the world, with many people swearing by the health benefits of a plant-based lifestyle. But does a vegan diet really improve your health? Click here to subscribe to our channel 👉🏽 This episode of CrowdScience (broadcast on radio in September 2019) explores the evidence about whether vegans are likely to live longer. With new, highly processed meat alternatives becoming increasingly available in supermarkets and restaurants menus, what effect are they having on our health? Presenter Anand Jagatia talks to the experts and joins listener Samantha in following a vegan diet. title: Why I’m Not Going Vegan | My Orthorexia Story
summary: I see a value in eating plant based, and I make an effort to eat more plant based ingredients and think beyond how I was raised. Benefits of Eating Plant based One of the big ones is sustainability and environmental considerations. Simply put, following any strict set of dietary rules is just not healthy for me based on my personal history with disordered eating.

description: Eating Disorder Support LINK: My favourite intuitive eating books in my amazon #affiliate shop: Get your copy of my book The Mindful Glow Cookbook HERE: Position of the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics: Vegetarian Diet – LINK HERE: As my channel as grown over the past year, I’ve learned a huge amount of my followers are plant based so I’ve been creating a lot of recipes to cater to my demographic. I see a value in eating plant based, and I make an effort to eat more plant based ingredients and think beyond how I was raised. Benefits of Eating Plant based One of the big ones is sustainability and environmental considerations. Recently a plant rich diet was recommended as the fourth most important strategy for global warming. So why don’t I just go vegan? Simply put, following any strict set of dietary rules is just not healthy for me based on my personal history with disordered eating. When I was 17, I was told by a homeopathic practitioners to go on a complete sugar detox which is when my diet when from being balanced to unbearably restrictive and “clean”. From there, most of my diet consisted of big salads with no dressing, steamed vegetables and sugar free jello. With orthorexia, it’s less about weight loss and the quantity of food and more about the quality. While I’m lucky to be alive, the most disturbing part of all this is that I was being praised for my malnourished body and my dedication to clean eating. The result of this style of eating is that it prevented me from wanting to go out with friends which enabled my disordered eating and depressive thoughts. Today, the popularity of clean eating and various wellness diets are becoming signs of moral superiority, economic privilege and class. First of all, I don’t want anyone to think that veganism causes orthorexia. However there’s a fine line between wanting to eat healthy and orthorexia. It all comes down to intentions. If you go out to dinner and the lack of control panics you, then you may have an issue. If your diet is starting to interfere with your emotional, psychological or social wellbeing, there’s a problem. What works for me is eating foods that make me feel satisfied and good without explicit labels. I eat how I want to eat in that moment, meal or day. I still believe that a lot of the benefits – for the environment, animals and our bodies that we see associated with plant based living can still be applied if we simply consume animal products in more modest ways. I’m not preaching for anyone to change their diet, you do what works for you. But again I want abbeys kitchen to be a respectful place for a variety of diets and everyone to eat in ways that feel intuitive and healthy to them. For more tips on staying healthy, recipes, dieting, and information fit for consumption by foodies everywhere stop by Abbey’s blog.

title: Nearly four in 10 Brits who have turned vegetarian or vegan have done so for a partner
summary: Research of 2,000 adults who have opted for a meat-free diet found 18 per cent made the switch to keep their other half happy. And 19 per cent wanted to support them in their quest for a healthier diet. But it isn’t just partners who influence eating habits, as 16 per cent made changes following requests from the children, and 19 per cent were persuaded by friends.
description: Nearly four in 10 Brits who have turned vegetarian or vegan have done so for a partner, a poll has found. Research of 2,000 adults who have opted for a meat-free diet found 18 per cent made the switch to keep their other half happy. And 19 per cent wanted to support them in their quest for a healthier diet. A third even claimed they would never have considered giving up meat if it wasn’t for the encouragement of their other half. But it isn’t just partners who influence eating habits, as 16 per cent made changes following requests from the children, and 19 per cent were persuaded by friends. It also emerged that eight in 10 found the change in diet easier to adapt to than they could have imagined, with 53 per cent saying they feel healthier and more energetic since adopting a plant-based diet. Tammy Fry, for international vegan food brand The Fry Family Food Co which carried out the study, said: “Our research shows that when it comes to trying out a plant-based diet, encouragement from partners, family and friends can be really helpful."

title: Zoë Harcombe on The Game Changers: Good Story, Unconvincing Argument
summary: The Game Changers bases its support of plant-based eating on three common arguments, Harcombe explains: 1. A healthy diet does not require supplements. A vegan diet requires supplements.
description: Zoë Harcombe, Ph.D., concedes The Game Changers tells “a good story,” but that’s as far as the 2019 documentary’s accolades should go, she suggests. In this presentation from Dec. 15, 2019, Harcombe offers an incisive and often humorous critique of the arguments the film makes in favor of a plant-based diet. The Game Changers bases its support of plant-based eating on three common arguments, Harcombe explains: 1. It’s healthier than non-vegan diets. 2. It’s better for the animals. 3. It’s better for the planet. Since the majority of the film is devoted to the nutrition argument, Harcombe focuses her attention there as well. After noting that, despite the movie’s claims, there is a dearth of scientific evidence for going plant-based, Harcombe turns with wry humor to the film’s burrito experiments. The science in these and the movie’s other experiments is deeply flawed, she claims. One experiment, which follows the Tennessee Titans as they turn to vegan eating, “is a great example that the standard American diet is rubbish,” Harcombe says. She argues any apparent benefits experienced by the athletes were probably attributable to their trading of junk food for whole foods. “Now the whole food just happens to be plant-based,” she notes, but “how good could they have been if their whole food diet had been animal-based?” she asks. Finally, Harcombe argues the film tries to gloss over the nutritional deficiencies in a vegan diet by suggesting everyone should take supplements, not only those who have gone plant-based. But those who follow a healthy diet of whole foods that include animal products do not need supplements, she claims: “A healthy diet provides the nutrients that we need. A healthy diet does not require supplements. A vegan diet requires supplements. De facto, a vegan diet is not healthy.” To read a complete transcript of the presentation, click here ( CrossFit® - (

title: ALL THE VEGAN FOOD + DESSERTS WE ATE IN LONDON! [ PIES, BURGERS, BUFFET & MORE! ]
summary: This is PART 1 of our Vegan Europe Eats Series! Vegan eats are what fuels our vegan travels and last summer in London we ate a ton of great vegan food! We had all kinds of different vegan eats!
description: This is PART 1 of our Vegan Europe Eats Series! Vegan eats are what fuels our vegan travels and last summer in London we ate a ton of great vegan food! We had all kinds of different vegan eats! A great vegan Asian buffet at "Tai Buffet", some traditional English pot pies from "Young Vegans" at Camden Market, an American style meal at "Unity Diner" (by Earthling Ed!) of a vegan cheeseburger, a vegan shrimp topped hot dog, and cheesey beefy nachos, some street food of a vegan bureka and a couple of vegan baos at The Borough Market, and a savory veggie filled stir-fry from "Wok to Walk" made up our main meals. I also indulged in a huge amount of delicious vegan donuts from "Dougnut Time" as well as a strawberry and chocolate gelato from "Amorino" and some cookies and cream cookie dough from "Naked Dough". We even grabbed a huge haul of vegan groceries from Whole Foods! Check out the full video for the full experience of all the things we ate, saw, and explored throughout London! title: Testing to prove supplements are real
summary: (26 Jul 2019) LEAD IN: Sales of plant based foods and herbal products is booming according to retail analysts. In this case it's coconut. It's the same for other ingredients.
description: (26 Jul 2019) LEAD IN: Sales of plant based foods and herbal products is booming according to retail analysts. It could mean we are looking for healthier, natural alternatives, but how do we know what we are buying is real? Each time we use a face cream, or a shampoo we take it on trust that the ingredients are correct, but now multinational corporations are seeking assurance from scientists at the UK's Royal Botanic Gardens. STORY-LINE: Fancy a glass of refreshing rosemary water, or perhaps something a little stronger, but unless your kitted out with a laboratory like this, you couldn't be sure the botanicals in your gin and tonic aren't just chemical flavourings. Retail analysts Kantar estimates 92% of plant-based meals consumed in the UK in 2018 are eaten by non-vegans, with 22 million people now identifying as flexitarian. According to Spins, a retail analyst that specialises in 'natural' products the US market for plant based foods is now worth $4.5 billion (USD) It's that sort of demand which has led the Royal Botanic Gardens at Kew to set up its Commercial Phytochemistry Unit. The unit is capitalising on Kew's world famous knowledge of plants to create a laboratory which accurately authenticates products for businesses and regulators. Here biologists test everything from medicinal plants to herbal shampoos. This shampoo range is based on a number of herbs and the company has engaged Kew to establish that the ingredients on the bottle match the contents inside it. In this case it's coconut. Ingredients are tested against Kew's own plant collection as well as others, using a machine called a mass spectrometer. These are the sort of machines sports regulators use to test athletes for doping. Research biologist Dr. Federico Padella explains: "We first want to separate them in the machine and then identify them. So we can be able to say yes this compound which is represented as a peak in a graph represents an amino acid X or amino acid Y, or any other chemical component that is abundant in the coconut." Identifying a coconut may seem very straightforward, but a multinational company which produces tones of a product each year probably won't see a fruit. They'll often use processed coconut which comes in bulk from suppliers all over world. It's the same for other ingredients. Scientists here say many of even the biggest companies don't have the expertise the botanists here have, especially if they're not used to dealing with natural products. It's also about quality control, being able to take different wholesale extracts and identify which one is best. "Each compound has a different what is called molecular weight, a different weight. So we separate them based on the weight in the machine and we can have the machine tells us what is exactly this molecular weight and when I get this molecule and I break it in pieces it will tell me like as a fingerprint. What kind of molecule is that based on the fragments," says Padella. The coconut goes into the mass spectrometer and Padella begins to analyse the peaks and troughs. The head of the phytochemistry lab is Kew's deputy director of science Professor Monique Simmonds. The growing use of herbal medicines and health supplements has created concern about their "potential harm when they are applied in an inadequate or uncontrolled manner" according to a 2013 UNESCO report. It said: "This is for example, the case of herbal medicine whose power is generally underestimated and insufficiently known." The Centre for Medical Cannabis claims this is equivalent in size to the whole of the UK's herbal supplement market. title: Where do vegans get their protein from? #shorts Vegan Questions & Answers in 60 Seconds or Less
summary: First up, where do vegans get their protein? How many people do you hear of dying from protein deficiencies? I don't think inadequate protein sources is a problem.
description: In this video series, I will be answering all the most common questions I get as a Vegan in 60 seconds or less! First up, where do vegans get their protein? Now, this is just a short list here, but the moral of the story is there is plenty of protein options to be had on a vegan diet. How many people do you hear of dying from protein deficiencies? Yeah ponder that for a minute and then think about all the stories you do hear about high cholesterol and heart disease... I don't think inadequate protein sources is a problem. I mean for me personally, I have only gained muscle since transitioning to a protein diet. So keep it sensible kids and eat a good and nutritious well-balanced diet regardless of your ethical choices... Also, please let me know what your questions are and maybe I will answer them in my next short! For more of us, check out our instagram: instagram.com/tlandry27 or visit the website at thehealthyhabit.co.uk #shorts #vegan #questionsandanswers #protein #veganfitness

title: Inside London’s first all vegan supermarket – where an egg substitute powder costs £7
summary: "Everything here is vegan. Meat substitutes are vegan versions of bacon, steaks, and sausages. We want the movement to move forward," co-founder Anderson Caicedo told Business Insider UK.
description: GreenBay is London’s first 100% vegan supermarket, which sells products that are sourced and selected from all over the world. "Everything here is vegan. If you come here obviously you don't have to worry about looking at the ingredients," co-founder Paula Alcalde told Business Insider UK. The supermarket has its own "vegan meat and cheese" section, where a block of cheese is sold for around £5. Meat substitutes are vegan versions of bacon, steaks, and sausages. There's also an egg substitute which costs £6.99. It's a powder you whisk in ice cold water. None of the products in the cleaning and body care aisle have been tested on animals. You can find a £2.49 4-pack of toilet paper or a £7.95 deodorant. "We are ethical. We really care about this thing. We want the movement to move forward," co-founder Anderson Caicedo told Business Insider UK. "We are doing this because we want to do it, rather than just making money." The supermarket opened in 2016 and will soon launch an online store. ----------------------------------------­­­­---------- Follow BI UK on Twitter: Follow BI UK On Facebook: Read more: ----------------------------------------­­­­---------- Business Insider UK is the largest business news site for British readers and viewers in the UK. title: Farmison - award winning online butcher
summary: Multi award-winning butcher Farmison & Co has selected leading food ERP software specialist SI (Systems Integration) to streamline its processes through full integration of its operations. Farmison & Co has seen significant growth since 2016 and is now in a position where there is a need for software to streamline processes within its operations. In addition, SI’s unique catering touchscreens will provide real-time order details and specifications direct to Farmison & Co’s master butchers, once the system is live.
description: Multi award-winning butcher Farmison & Co has selected leading food ERP software specialist SI (Systems Integration) to streamline its processes through full integration of its operations. Farmison & Co has seen significant growth since 2016 and is now in a position where there is a need for software to streamline processes within its operations. Speaking about its vision and why it chose SI, Liam Duggan, Finance Manager said: “We are renowned for delivering excellent products and services to our customers, and our master butchery. Now, this winning combination has provided us with the ideal opportunity to underpin our expansion plans by investing in a technology platform. We initially spoke to SI because of the company’s outstanding reputation for delivering software within the meat processing sector and their in depth understanding of the pressure points faced by catering butchers. They recommended we take a modular approach and following a full audit of our operations, SI documented an end-to-end process map, that clearly detailed how their software would be integrated to provide us with ‘one version of the truth’.” As part of the integration process, SI will be implementing its modular food software to provide digital control from raw material intake, line production and through to stock management, despatch for customer fulfilment on orders. In addition, SI’s unique catering touchscreens will provide real-time order details and specifications direct to Farmison & Co’s master butchers, once the system is live. Talking about the new SI solution, SI Sales Manager, Phil Linton said: “Once complete, Farmison & Co will have upgraded their previous disparate systems with a single platform that will track the entire end-to-end process. Furthermore, Farmison & Co’s master butchers will have the confidence that, through SI’s catering butchery touchscreens, they will always be working to up to the minute order details and specifications whenever they are cutting meat. “Not only that, the team will be able to interrogate data at any time to improve forecasting, enabling them to meet the push and pull of optimal stock management v sales and spikes in demand. We will even be integrating our food ERP with their accounts system and bespoke e-commerce platform.” “SI is proud to now be working with Farmison & Co and its award-winning team. It’s a further endorsement of our growing reputation as the leading provider of software solutions to the catering butcher segment of the meat processing industry.

description: Get the BEST antibiotic-free, pasture-raised pork from Thrive Market: I headed to the meat section of the grocery store to show you what to look for when you buy beef, chicken, pork, and turkey. There are a lot of options, but conventional meat comes from factory farms and eats a diet of GMO corn and soy, this is not what you want to buy. FOr beef, grass-fed is by far the best choice. For chicken, always buy organic, or if you can go to a local co-op try to look for pasture raised. Pork is the toughest to buy, almost all of it is factory farmed, there is no organic or pasture raised pork. For ground turkey, look for organic and stay away from white meat, it's too lean. description: Hubbub investigates the relationship between fitness, strength and the myth that protein needs to come from meat. The team head down to a gym to meet Adam, our guru who specialises on getting men and women fit, strong and healthy, on a plant based diet, to share alternative foods that are high in protein. We also introduce Hubbub's new 'Meat You Match' trial, challenging male gym users to replace half of their animal-based protein with plant-based protein! Stay tuned to see how they get on. So why are we looking at meat in the first place? Britons eat 84.2 kg of meat per person per year and global meat and dairy production is set to double by 2050 because of a growing population and already the industry contributes 14.5% to the total greenhouse gas emissions. As well as that there are increasing concerns about the effect eating too much meat can have on your health with links to diabetes and heart disease. description: It's time to hit the grocery store and show you guys all of the options of chicken and what you should buy and avoid. There are many different options wen it comes to buying chicken, and it's hard to understand what it all means. Which is why I want to break it down for you. Pay no attention to marketing terms like hormone free and cage free, they mean nothing! But do pay attention to air chilled, that is really important because water chilled chicken is is bland and chewy because of the added moisture. Fresh chicken is important too, it means the meat has never been frozen and that will produce the most optimal texture. You really want to buy organic chicken at the grocery store, the stamp means a lot. The chickens have a non-GMO organic vegetarian feed and have access to the outdoors. Below are the most common store bought organic brands you can find at the stores. I hope this video helps you pick the best chicken and understand where it comes from. description: Brooks Bell, the Colonoscopy Enthusiast and Colorectal Cancer Alliance Board Member, goes shopping before a follow-up colonoscopy. What are some of the best things to buy at the grocery store when prepping for a colonoscopy? Brooks helps build your shopping list! In January 2019, Bell was diagnosed with stage-III colon cancer at age 38. She underwent six months of surgeries and chemotherapy. During that time, she learned of the shocking rise of colorectal cancer in young people, which inspired her to share her story, establish an awareness campaign, and start an initiative and event to encourage colorectal cancer screening. Colorectal cancer screening is the No. 1 way to prevent colorectal cancer or detect it early, when it's most treatable. All average-risk adults should begin screening at age 45.
